chunk_summary: Micronutrient replacement in gluten-free diets for celiac disease should address deficiencies proactively, emphasizing whole grains, fiber, calcium, and vitamin D while avoiding over-reliance on processed gluten-free products.
learning_objectives: Understand the common micronutrient deficiencies in pediatric celiac disease and their management.
Learn dietary strategies to improve nutrient density in gluten-free eating patterns.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and reassessment in cases of persistent symptoms or poor growth.
Develop practical plans for cross-contact prevention and dietary adherence in shared environments.
key_facts: Iron, folate, vitamin B12, calcium, vitamin D, zinc, magnesium, and fiber are commonly deficient in gluten-free diets, especially after prolonged malabsorption.
Therapeutic oral elemental iron dosing is recommended for iron deficiency anemia, with follow-up of hemoglobin (Hb) and ferritin levels.
India-adapted gluten-free staples include rice, jowar, bajra, ragi, and other millets, along with pulses, vegetables, and fruits for fiber.
Calcium and vitamin D intake can be supported with milk/curd, ragi, sesame, fortified foods, or supplements if needed.
Lactose-reduced dairy or calcium-fortified substitutes are recommended for persistent lactose intolerance post-diagnosis.
reference_quote: At diagnosis, treat confirmed deficiencies rather than relying on dietary advice alone. Iron deficiency anemia should receive therapeutic oral elemental iron dosing with follow-up of Hb and ferritin.
document_key_topics: Follow-up and reassessment in celiac disease management
